eras.name: Licchavi Golden Age
eras.description: A period characterized by the refinement of weaving and the use of fine muslins. Clothing was draped and influenced by Gupta-era aesthetics, often featuring intricate gold jewelry and floral garlands.
eras.aiImagePrompt: Historical illustration of a 7th-century Nepali Licchavi noblewoman wearing a finely draped white muslin antariya with gold borders, multiple strands of turquoise and coral necklaces, and hair adorned with fresh jasmine, standing against a backdrop of ancient stone pagoda architecture.

eras.name: Malla Dynasty Craftsmanship
eras.description: The era where Newari craftsmanship peaked, introducing structured garments for the elite. Use of heavy brocades and the development of the Haku Patasi style among the Newar community became prominent.
eras.aiImagePrompt: A Newar woman from the 15th century Malla period wearing a Haku Patasi (black sari with red border) draped in multiple layers, with a white shawl (Ga) and heavy traditional Newari gold jewelry (Tayoo), set in a Kathmandu Durbar Square courtyard.

eras.name: Rana Regency Westernization
eras.description: A period of heavy European influence where the ruling elite adopted Victorian and Edwardian silhouettes. High-ranking officials wore military uniforms with heavy gold embroidery and ostrich feather plumes.
eras.aiImagePrompt: A 19th-century Nepali Rana general in a dark green military tunic heavily embroidered with gold bullion filigree, wearing a Shreepech crown with bird-of-paradise plumes, blending Victorian military style with Himalayan royalty.

eras.name: Panchayat Era Uniformity
eras.description: A period focused on national identity where the Daura Suruwal and Dhaka Topi were mandated as official national dress for men. This era solidified the Dhaka fabric as a symbol of Nepalese heritage.
eras.aiImagePrompt: A mid-20th-century Nepali man wearing a grey Daura Suruwal with a distinct geometric patterned Dhaka Topi, standing in a terraced mountain village, representing the standardized national identity.

eras.name: Contemporary Fusion
eras.description: Integration of global fashion trends with traditional textiles. Young designers are repurposing Dhaka and Pashmina into modern silhouettes like blazers, jumpsuits, and high-fashion evening wear.
eras.aiImagePrompt: A professional fashion photograph of a modern Nepali model wearing a sharp-cut tailored blazer made of colorful geometric Palpali Dhaka fabric, paired with high-waisted trousers and minimalist gold accessories, urban Kathmandu background.

textiles.name: Dhaka
textiles.description: A hand-loomed cotton fabric featuring intricate geometric patterns, traditionally produced in Palpa and Tehrathum.

textiles.name: Pashmina
textiles.description: Ultra-fine wool harvested from the undercoat of Changthangi goats found in the high Himalayas.

textiles.name: Himalayan Hemp
textiles.description: Durable, eco-friendly fiber derived from wild cannabis plants, used for rugged outerwear and accessories.

climate.fashionImplication: Extreme altitudinal variation necessitates a layering system, transitioning from light cottons in the Terai plains to heavy wools and furs in the Himalayan highlands.
